sections.title: Welcome to This Technical Specification
sections.fullText: This document is your complete guide to the audiovisual and technical systems being designed for Lightheart, Dublin's new immersive art space. Whether you're a project stakeholder, facilities manager, vendor, or technical contractor, this guide will help you understand what we're building, why we've made specific choices, and what happens next.

sections.title: What This Document Contains
sections.fullText: This specification covers every aspect of the two-room immersive installation, from the projectors that will fill the walls with light to the servers that will power the experience. We've organized it into logical sections that build upon each other–starting with the big picture and progressively diving deeper into technical details.

sections.title: How to Use This Document
sections.fullText:
For decision-makers: Start with the Technical Direction Overview and Budget Summary to understand the scope and investment

For technical contractors: Focus on the specific sections relevant to your trade (Projectors, Audio, Network, HVAC)

For vendors: Review the Contractors/Vendors section and your relevant technical specifications

For facilities teams: Pay special attention to the Server Room and HVAC sections for ongoing operations

sections.title: Project Summary
sections.fullText:
This project defines, designs, and costs a robust audiovisual and systems solution for two immersive rooms through a focused, high-impact pre-production package. The work includes an options-based projection and playback design, a power and electrical load review, an assessment of the weight load on the roof, and a clear systems architecture. The structure supports confident, short-timeline decision-making, vendor pricing, technical coordination, and the long-term operability of a permanent installation.

Project Lead(s): Krisjanis Berzins - kris@idirnet.com

sections.title: Core Objectives
sections.fullText:
Deliver a complete AV and systems specification with clear performance tiers and trade-offs.

Confirm technical feasibility through coordinated design intent for mounts, rigging requirements, and building services inputs.

Produce a practical server, playback, and network architecture that is supportable and scalable.

Provide a power and electrical load review to support M&E planning and reduce late-stage rework.

Support vendor engagement and comparable pricing to ring-fence a realistic budget envelope.

sections.title: Approach & Deliverables
sections.fullText:
The project is structured in three phases, moving from definition to buildable technical design, and finally to vendor-ready costing and budget allocation.

Phase

Purpose

Key Deliverables

Phase 1: Definition and Technical Discovery

Translate the current scope into a clearly defined technical challenge with quantifiable requirements and aligned constraints.

*   Scope confirmation pack (room details, audience movement, maintenance needs, etc.)

 *   Projection strategy options (Wall-only/Wall plus floor, redundancy, lensing)

 *   Assumptions and risk register

Phase 2: System Design and Engineering Coordination

Create a detailed technical design that can be accurately priced by vendors and efficiently supported by Lightheart and building services teams.

*   Projection system study and specification by room (tiers, brightness, resolution, mounting, mapping workflow)

*   Basic previz output

 *   Mounting and rigging design intent package with Motion detection and sensing options (if required)

 *   Server and playback architecture (synchronisation, redundancy, GPU/CPU, networking)

 *   Systems architecture overview (Network, control layers, audio integration, IT requirements)

 *   Power and electrical load review (Energy use, heat/ventilation inputs, circuiting approach)

Phase 3: Vendor Coordination, Costing, and Budget Allocation

Convert the design into costed and comparable solutions, supported by a clear budget structure for decision-making and procurement planning.

*   Vendor engagement pack (Scope, drawings, pricing template)

 *   Vendor communication and technical Q&A

 *   Costed options comparison (Room-by-room across performance tiers)

 *   Budget allocation summary (Recommended envelope, contingency, long-lead items)

 *   Handover package for the next stage (Consolidated drawings, specs, vendor feedback)

sections.title: Key Technical Decisions
sections.fullText:
Signal Transport Strategy

The project requires 4:4:4 @ 60Hz 16:10 signal transport for both 4K and 2K systems. Options under evaluation include:

• DVIGear DVI-7380 DP to DP 4K over OS2 Fiber (22x sets)

• HDBaseT for shorter runs (cost-effective alternative)

• ST2110/IPMX [Professional media over IP standards for broadcast] for future-proofing (under consideration)

Audio-Visual Integration

Important: L-Acoustics L-ISA uses Milan-AVB protocol, not Dante. Pixera does not support AVB natively. Integration requires either a hardware converter or sync-based separation with Meinberg sync generator.

sections.title: Section 3: Projectors
sections.fullText:
What This Section Is About

Projectors are the most visible element of this installation–they literally create what visitors see. This section details every projector in the system: the high-end 4K wall projectors, the floor projection units, the special ultra-short-throw lenses, and why we selected Barco over alternatives from Panasonic and Epson.

Why It Matters

Projection quality defines the visitor experience. Poor brightness, visible pixels, or misaligned images break immersion. We've specified professional-grade equipment capable of edge-blending seamlessly across multiple surfaces while delivering the brightness needed for an art space environment.

We've specified professional-grade equipment capable of edge-blending seamlessly across multiple surfaces while delivering the brightness needed for an art space environment.

sections.title: Resolution Architecture
sections.fullText:
All 4K projectors specified utilize WQUXGA (3840×2400) resolution at 16:10 aspect ratio. This is achieved via Barco SuperShift™ technology: a 4-way pixel-shifting optical actuator on a 0.8" WUXGA DMD (Digital Micromirror Device - chip with microscopic mirrors for projection) that renders 9.2 million discrete pixels (vs. Epson's 2-way 4K Enhancement yielding 4.6 million pixels).

Bandwidth Requirement: 15.9 Gbps per channel ( uncompressed 4:4:4 @ 60Hz)–11.1% higher data density than standard 4K UHD, necessitating DisplayPort 1.4 transport (32.4 Gbps capacity) over HDMI 2.0 (18 Gbps ceiling).

sections.title: Room 1 Walls (Primary Gallery)
sections.fullText:
14× Barco I600‑4K10 (10,000 ISO lm, WQUXGA) with ILD 0.37 UST lenses

Optical System: Fixed 0.37:1 ultra-short-throw periscope lens (6.2kg optical assembly)

Rigging Weight: 29.8 kg per unit (23.6 kg body + 6.2 kg lens assembly) (14× = 417.2 kg)

Power: 860W per unit (14× = 12,040W / 55A total)

Thermal: 2,900 BTU/h per unit (14× = 40,600 BTU/h)

Acoustic: 40 dB (normal operation)

Cost: €490,000 (equipment) + €14,700 (signal transport) = €504,700

sections.title: Tier 1 (10K Lumen 4K UST):
sections.fullText:
Panasonic PT-REQ10 + ET-C1U100: 1.07× optical zoom (0.308–0.330:1) offers mounting flexibility, but total rigging weight 31.7kg (+2kg vs Barco) and lacks native 16:10 WQUXGA support without scaling.

Epson EB-PU2010B + ELPLX01S: 20.3kg total weight (lightest option) and 35 dB quiet operation, but 2-way pixel shift (4.6M pixels) insufficient for sharp generative text/data viz. Native contrast inferior to DLP.

Tier 2 (8K Lumen WUXGA):

Panasonic: PT-REZ80 Projector + ET-C1W300 Short-Throw Zoom Lens.

Match Note: This lens provides a 0.55–0.69:1 throw ratio. It comfortably reaches the Barco's 0.65 minimum requirement while offering room to go wider if needed.

Epson: EB-PQ2008B Projector + ELPLU04 Short-Throw Zoom Lens.

Match Note: The Epson lens provides an approximate 0.64–0.77:1 WUXGA-equivalent throw ratio on this chassis, serving as a nearly perfect 1:1 match for the Barco G-lens.

Selection Rationale: Barco specified for true 4-way pixel shift resolution (critical for CAD/engineering viz), native 16:10 aspect ratio (matching room geometry), and SuperShift optical stability. Accepted trade-offs: 40 dB acoustic footprint (mitigated by immersive audio masking) and 6.2kg UST lens weight (requires reinforced rigging).

sections.title: Why Barco over Panasonic/Epson (detailed):
sections.fullText:
While Panasonic and Epson are exceptional in their respective classes, Barco projectors are often specified for the highest-end professional environments (like dark rides, advanced simulation, and massive live events) due to a specific set of architectural, software, and support advantages.

Here is a breakdown of Barco’s unique selling points and where they hold superiority over their Panasonic and Epson counterparts:

1. The Barco Pulse Ecosystem & Single Step Processing (SSP)

The most significant technological advantage Barco holds is its proprietary Pulse operating system and its Single Step Processing (SSP) architecture.

Zero-Latency Geometry: When you apply complex geometric warping, edge blending, and 4K pixel-shifting on standard projectors, the internal electronics process these tasks sequentially, which inherently adds input lag. Barco's SSP executes all of these heavy processing tasks in parallel within a single frame. This results in near-zero latency, which is an absolute necessity for simulators or live events using real-time tracking.

DynaBlack: Competing dynamic contrast systems often crush highlight details when dimming the laser for dark scenes. Barco’s DynaBlack uses the FPGA processing power of the Pulse ecosystem to analyze the image frame-by-frame and enhance contrast at the pixel level without adding latency or image artifacts.

2. Massive Lens Shift and the "Optical Axis" Secret Barco's optical pathways are engineered to provide an extraordinary range of lens shift –sometimes reaching up to 130% vertically and 50% horizontally, depending on the specific lens. On the I600 series, you can achieve up to 110% vertical and 30% horizontal shift.

The Overlooked Detail: When comparing spec sheets, Barco calculates its lens shift percentage using the absolute center of the lens (the optical axis) as its 0% reference point. Many competitors use the bottom or top edge of the screen as their 0% baseline. Because of this calculation difference, Barco's shift capabilities in the real world often provide significantly more physical placement flexibility than a competitor's equivalent percentage implies.

3. The "Advance Swap" Service Model

In commercial integration, downtime equals lost revenue. Standard warranties from Panasonic and Epson often rely on a "return to base" repair model or dispatching an engineer for an on-site fix, which can leave a venue without a projector for days or weeks.

Barco operates a highly aggressive "advance swap" service model. If a projector fails, Barco immediately ships a like-for-like replacement from a regional swap pool, usually arriving the next business day. You install the replacement, keep it permanently, and send the broken unit back. This limits downtime to a matter of hours.

4. P7 RealColor Factory Characterization

Edge-blending multiple projectors from Epson or Panasonic usually requires an integrator to manually tweak color values or use external cameras to achieve uniform color across the canvas.

Barco utilizes P7 RealColor technology. Every single Barco projector is optically and electrically characterized at the factory before it goes in the box. The projector internalizes its exact color coordinates, meaning an integrator can simply input the desired white point and color space (like Rec. 709) into the software, and multiple projectors will automatically and perfectly match each other with minimal manual calibration.

sections.title: Section 4: Signal Transport
sections.fullText:
What This Section Is About

Getting video from servers to projectors sounds simple, but when you're pushing uncompressed 4K signals over 50-meter distances, it becomes a significant engineering challenge. This section explains how video signals travel through the system and why we selected fiber optic transport.

Why It Matters

Signal quality directly impacts image quality. Compressed or degraded signals result in banding, color inaccuracy, and soft details. Our specification requires uncompressed 4:4:4 signals at 60Hz–this demands serious bandwidth that standard HDMI or HDBaseT cannot reliably provide over the distances involved.

sections.title: Section 5: Surface Treatment
sections.fullText:
What This Section Is About

Even the best projectors need appropriate surfaces to shine on. This section covers the special paint systems being specified for walls and floors–why standard white paint won't work, and how the right surface treatment prevents "hot-spotting" (bright spots where projector beams overlap).

Why It Matters

Projection surfaces are often overlooked, but they're critical to image quality. The wrong surface creates uneven brightness, visible seams between projectors, and colors that shift across the room. We've specified low-gain grey formulations optimized for multi-projector blending.

sections.title: Section 6: Sensors
sections.fullText:
What This Section Is About

Immersive art often responds to visitors. This section covers the camera system that enables this interactivity–44 depth-sensing cameras that can track movement, detect presence, and trigger responsive content.

Why It Matters

These cameras are the venue's "eyes"–they enable artworks to react to visitors, creating dynamic experiences that change based on who is present and what they do. However, 44 cameras generating high-bandwidth video creates significant technical challenges.

sections.title: Section 7: Network
sections.fullText:
What This Section Is About

This venue contains multiple complex systems that all need to communicate–video, audio, sensors, control, and file sharing. This section explains how we've designed five separate networks to keep these systems running smoothly without interfering with each other.

Why It Matters

Network problems manifest as audio dropouts, video glitches, or unresponsive sensors. By segregating traffic into separate physical and logical networks, we prevent problems in one system from affecting others. The audio network, for example, has guaranteed bandwidth that can't be disrupted by someone uploading large files.

sections.title: Critical Segregation Rules
sections.fullText:
AVB Isolation: Milan traffic (Layer 2 MAC-based) must not traverse standard IP routers; requires certified AVB switches (LS10) with Stream Reservation Protocol (SRP)

Dante/AVB Separation: If Dante bridging implemented (Architecture A only), physically separate switch fabrics or strictly isolated VLANs required; Dante utilizes DSCP QoS prioritization while AVB requires hardware-level bandwidth reservation

Pixera Internal: Server cluster multicast traffic (5GB/s+ internal file distribution) isolated on dedicated unmanaged switches with IGMP Snooping–never shared with audio networks

Sensor/Video Isolation: Bursty TCP/IP camera traffic physically separated from deterministic AVB streams to prevent queue congestion and clock jitter

sections.title: Section 8: Audio System / L-ISA
sections.fullText:
What This Section Is About

Sound is half the immersive experience. This section details the L-Acoustics L-ISA spatial audio system–71 speakers positioned around and above visitors to create three-dimensional soundscapes that move and breathe with the content.

Why It Matters

Traditional stereo or surround sound places audio in fixed positions. L-ISA is "object-based"–sounds exist as points in 3D space that can move freely around, above, and through the listening area. This creates the sense of being "inside" the sound rather than just hearing it from speakers.

sections.title: System Overview

The system utilizes object-based spatial audio processing with Milan-AVB networking protocol for reliable, low-latency audio distribution.

sections.title: Network Architecture & Protocols
sections.fullText:
Audio Network Protocol

sections.title: Section 9: Audio Bridging Architectures
sections.fullText:
What This Section Is About

The chosen audio system uses Milan-AVB protocol. The media servers don't natively support it. This section explains the bridging architectures we're evaluating to connect these systems, and the trade-offs involved in each approach.

Why It Matters

Audio and video must stay synchronized. If the media server and audio system can't communicate directly, we need a bridge–and different bridge options introduce different amounts of delay, cost, and complexity. Getting this wrong means lip-sync problems where sound doesn't match what's on screen.

sections.title: Executive Summary
sections.fullText:
The Lightheart Immersive Experience audio system is built on Milan-AVB (Audio Video Bridging) as its native network audio protocol. This document outlines the bridging architecture, protocol compatibility considerations, and integration points with other building systems.

CRITICAL NOTE: L-ISA Processor II does NOT support Dante natively. The system is AVB/Milan only. This creates a protocol boundary that requires careful consideration when integrating with building systems or media servers.

sections.title: Protocol Overview
sections.fullText:
Milan-AVB (Primary Protocol)

Milan is a certification program built on the IEEE AVB standards, ensuring interoperability between certified devices. The L-Acoustics system uses Milan-AVB for all audio transport.

Standard

IEEE 802.1BA-2011 + Avnu ProAV 1.1

Bandwidth

1 Gbit/s per link

Latency

2 ms maximum (guaranteed)

Redundancy

Seamless failover (primary/secondary networks)

Channels per Stream

Up to 8 channels

Max Streams (LS10)

150 streams

Clocking

gPTP (IEEE 802.1AS) - precision time protocol

Key AVB/Milan Advantages

Guaranteed bandwidth allocation prevents audio dropouts

Deterministic latency ensures consistent timing

Automatic device discovery and configuration

Seamless redundancy with sub-second failover

No manual network QoS configuration required

sections.title: Integration Options
sections.fullText:
Option 1: AVB-to-Dante Bridge/Converter

A hardware converter device can bridge between AVB and Dante networks. This is the recommended approach if Dante integration is required.

Pros: Maintains audio quality, supports full channel count

Cons: Additional cost, potential latency increase, single point of failure

Option 2: MADI Interface

L-ISA Processor II supports MADI (Multichannel Audio Digital Interface) via BNC connectors. MADI can be used to connect to non-AVB equipment.

Pros: Low latency, high channel count (64 channels @ 48kHz)

Cons: Requires MADI-equipped devices, point-to-point only

Option 3: AES/EBU Digital Audio

L-ISA Processor II and LA7.16i amplifiers support AES/EBU (AES3) digital audio via XLR connectors for limited channel counts.

Pros: Universal compatibility, simple wiring

Cons: Limited to 2 channels per connection, requires multiple cables

sections.title: Troubleshooting & Diagnostics
sections.fullText:
Common Issues

No Audio

Check AVB stream subscription, verify primary/secondary links

Audio Dropouts

Check for network loops, verify cable quality

Sync Issues

Verify Grandmaster clock, check gPTP status

High Latency

Check for non-AVB switches in path, verify cable lengths

Diagnostic Tools

LA Network Manager (L-Acoustics) - amplifier monitoring

L-ISA Controller - processor configuration and monitoring

AVB Protocol Analyzer (if available) - network diagnostics

Switch web interface - port status and statistics

Recommendations

1. Confirm Pixera AVB Support
Prioritize confirming whether Pixera supports AVB natively. This is the single most important factor in determining the bridging architecture.

2. Define Network Handover Point
Work with the building IT contractor to define the physical and logical handover point between AVB and building networks.

3. Budget for Converter (if needed)
If Pixera does not support AVB, budget €2,000-6,000 for an AVB-to-Dante/MADI converter.

4. Include Sync Generator
Ensure the sync generator is quoted and included for proper A/V synchronization.

sections.title: Section 10: Latency Budgeting
sections.fullText:
What This Section Is About

When sound and video travel through complex systems, delays add up. This section calculates the total time from when a signal enters the system to when it emerges from speakers–and confirms we stay within acceptable limits for synchronized playback.

Why It Matters

Humans notice when audio and video don't match. In film, the standard is ±20ms–beyond that, viewers perceive lip-sync errors. Our immersive content needs tight synchronization so that visual events and their accompanying sounds occur simultaneously.

subsections.title: What This Section Is About
subsections.text: When sound and video travel through complex systems, delays add up. This section calculates the total time from when a signal enters the system to when it emerges from speakers–and confirms we stay within acceptable limits for synchronized playback.

sections.title: Executive Summary
sections.fullText:
This document provides a comprehensive latency budget for the Lightheart Immersive Experience audio system. The analysis covers all processing stages from audio source to speaker output, ensuring lip-sync accuracy with video content.

TARGET: Total end-to-end latency should not exceed 40ms to maintain perceptual synchronization with video. The L-Acoustics system is well within this budget.

sections.fullText:
Perceptual Thresholds

< 10 ms

Imperceptible - professional live sound standard

10-20 ms

Barely perceptible - acceptable for most applications

20-40 ms

Slightly perceptible - acceptable for video sync

> 40 ms

Noticeable delay - lip-sync issues may occur

> 100 ms

Unacceptable - clear echo/delay effect

sections.title: Variable Latency Factors
sections.fullText:
Network-Induced Variation

AVB/Milan guarantees maximum latency, but actual latency may vary:

Minimum

~1 ms (ideal conditions, short cables)

Typical

1.5-2 ms (normal operation)

Maximum

2 ms (guaranteed by AVB standard)

Jitter

< 1 ms (phase-locked to gPTP)

Distance-Related Latency

Physical distance between speakers and listeners adds acoustic latency:

2 meters

5.8 ms

5 meters

14.5 ms

10 meters

29 ms

20 meters

58 ms

NOTE: Listener position affects perceived timing. Front vs. rear speakers may have different effective latencies based on listener distance.

sections.title: Audio-Video Synchronization
sections.fullText:
Synchronization Chain

For sample-accurate A/V sync, all systems must share a common clock reference:

1. Sync Generator

Meinberg (TBC) provides house sync/TC

2. L-ISA Processor

Receives word clock or timecode

3. Media Server

Pixera receives same sync reference

4. Video Displays

Locked to house sync

Latency Compensation

If video processing introduces latency, audio can be delayed to match:

LA7.16i output delay: 0-1000 ms configurable

L-ISA Processor delay: Available per output

Typical video latency: 1-3 frames (16-50ms @ 60Hz)

ACTION REQUIRED: Measure actual video latency from media server to display to determine if audio delay compensation is needed.

Latency Measurement

Measurement Methods

Method 1

Impulse response measurement (swept sine)

Method 2

Loopback test with reference microphone

Method 3

Dedicated latency analyzer (e.g., Smaart)

Method 4

Oscilloscope with test tone generator

Recommended Test Procedure

1. System Setup
Configure system for normal operation with all processing enabled.

2. Reference Measurement
Measure latency at reference position (center of listening area).

3. Multi-Point Measurement
Measure at multiple positions to verify consistency.

4. Document Results
Record measurements for future reference and troubleshooting.

sections.title: Recommendations
sections.fullText:
1. Use Standard Latency Mode
The 3.84ms amplifier latency provides optimal audio quality. Low latency mode (1.18ms) is not necessary given the comfortable headroom.

2. Implement Sync Generator
Ensure the Meinberg sync generator is installed and configured for sample-accurate A/V synchronization.

3. Measure Video Latency
Determine actual video latency to calculate if audio delay compensation is required.

4. Document Configuration
Record all latency-related settings for future reference and maintenance.

sections.title: Section 11: Server Room
sections.fullText:
What This Section Is About

Behind every immersive experience is a room full of equipment that makes it possible. This section details the server room infrastructure–the racks, power protection, media servers, network hardware, and cabling that form the technical backbone of the venue.

Why It Matters

This equipment runs continuously during operating hours. It must be reliable, supportable, and adequately protected. A server room failure means the venue can't operate. We've specified enterprise-grade equipment with redundancy for critical systems.

sections.title: Section 12: Server Room & General HVAC
sections.fullText:
Executive Summary

This section details the thermal load and cooling requirements for the Lightheart Immersive Experience. Because electronics and heat degrade performance and lifespan, the HVAC system must handle peak loads during hot weather while maintaining headroom for future expansion.

Critical Gap: Heat output values for L-Acoustics audio equipment are currently unavailable. The HVAC contractor must obtain this data directly from Audiotek/L-Acoustics to finalize sizing.

Recommendation: Size Server Room HVAC for ~45,000 to 50,000 BTU/h. Gallery HVAC must separately account for ~83,000 BTU/h of projector heat. Note that while projectors are in the gallery spaces, they contribute heavily to the building's overall thermal load.

sections.title: 3. Final HVAC Sizing & Environmental Requirements
sections.fullText:
Environmental & Operating Specs

Target Room Temp: 18-24°C (64-75°F) for maximum longevity. (Maximum allowable is 27°C / 80°F).

Humidity: 40-60% (non-condensing).

Air Changes: Minimum 15-20 ACH recommended.

Acoustics: The HVAC must utilize low-velocity ductwork and sound attenuators to hit a target rating of NC-35 or better in adjacent gallery spaces.

Airflow & Racking

LA7.16i amplifiers require front-to-rear forced-air cooling via 5 DSP-controlled fans.

All amplifiers must be front-facing.

Racks require a minimum of 100mm clearance on both the front and rear.

sections.fullText:

Contact Audiotek/L-Acoustics: Procure exact BTU/h outputs for the LA7.16i (at both 15-20% load and maximum load), the L-ISA Processor II, and the LS10 switches.

Clear vendor relationships prevent confusion and delays. Knowing who to contact for each system, when quotes expire, and what decisions remain helps keep the project moving forward efficiently.
